label: ritual_bacabs::chay_cnidoscolus
text: Chay (Cnidoscolus aconitifolius) — Tree Spinach / Chaya. From Yucatec Maya oral tradition documented by Roys and subsequent ethnobotanists. Maya healer's description: U ti'al u yilaj ti' máak ku k'iinikil le che'o' — 'In order to see to a sick person, this tree.' Chay is prepared by boiling leaves for five or more minutes before consumption — the Maya knowledge that raw leaves are harmful (toxic compounds activated by cooking) represents precise pharmacological understanding without knowledge of chemistry. Used for: blood building (ku yantal k'iik'), fever reduction (ku jaats' k'aak'), digestive complaints, and as a general strengthening food for the weakened. The Maya medical tradition distinguishes between chay prepared as medicine (concentrated decoction, more leaves, longer boiling) and chay prepared as food (standard boiling of leaves, eaten as vegetable). Active research: extraordinary nutritional density — 3x vitamin C of orange, 25x iron of banana, 7g protein per 100g cooked leaves. Anti-diabetic activity in preliminary studies.

label: ritual_bacabs::copal_pom_bursera
text: Pom (Bursera copallifera / B. bipinnata) — Maya Copal Resin. Ritual of the Bacabs (Roys trans.): 'Ix-pom, ix-cacao, ix-ya-che — copal, cacao tree, sapodilla tree — these are the medicines of the great healers.' Copal (pom) was both ritual and medicinal in Maya culture — a distinction the tradition did not draw as sharply as Western medicine. Medicinal uses documented: antimicrobial wound treatment (resin applied to infected wounds), pain relief (smoke inhalation for headache), respiratory conditions (burning in enclosed space, inhaled). Maya copal is a genuinely independent discovery parallel to Cobo's documentation of copal in the Andean tradition and to Greek/Roman use of Boswellia (frankincense, same plant family — Burseraceae). Cross-tradition convergence: THREE independent traditions (Maya, Andean/Cobo, Greek/Roman) independently documented antimicrobial and wound-healing properties of Burseraceae family resins. Modern: Bursera species contain compounds with documented antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity.

label: ritual_bacabs::balche_lonchocarpus
text: Balché (Lonchocarpus violaceus / L. longistylus) — Sacred Maya Ritual Drink. Ritual of the Bacabs, Healing incantation text: U ts'o'ok u bin balché ti' noj nah... ka xoc-lah u k'ab u yich balché. 'The balché went to the great house... the hands and face of the balché were counted.' Balché is a ritual fermented drink made from the bark of Lonchocarpus soaked in honey water and fermented. Used in Maya healing ceremonies by the h-men (healer-priest) for purification, vision, and diagnosis of disease cause. Pharmacological significance: Lonchocarpus species contain rotenone and related flavonoids with documented MAO-inhibiting (monoamine oxidase inhibiting) activity. This creates a remarkable parallel with the Amazonian ayahuasca combination — both traditions independently discovered that combining a MAO-inhibiting plant with a fermented preparation produces altered states useful for healing. The convergent discovery of MAO inhibition in healing contexts, independently in Mesoamerica and the Amazon, is one of the strongest signals in the convergence database.

label: ritual_bacabs::xiu_healing_plants_general
text: Xiu (Plants in general) — Maya concept of plant medicine. Roys (1965), Ritual of the Bacabs introduction: 'The Maya h-men (healer-priest) was simultaneously a botanist, pharmacologist, priest, and diviner. The separation of these roles is a European imposition on a system that understood them as unified.' Maya disease classification: diseases sent by supernatural forces (u kinil ik — diseases from the winds/spirits) vs. diseases from natural causes (u kinil tan — diseases from the body itself). Plant medicines used for both categories, but ritual and incantation accompany treatment of supernatural diseases. Key healing plants in the Ritual of the Bacabs beyond those catalogued separately: sakam (white flower tree, possibly Plumeria), tancás (identified as Brassavola nodosa orchid, used for fever), kanlol (yellow flower, multiple species), xikin-che (ear tree, Enterolobium cyclocarpum, for ear conditions). The Yucatan Peninsula's botanical diversity — at the junction of Mexican highland and Caribbean lowland flora — produced a pharmacopeia substantially different from Aztec medicine despite proximity.

label: ritual_bacabs::xtabay_plumeria
text: Nicté (Plumeria rubra / P. obtusa) — Frangipani / White Flower. Ritual of the Bacabs and related documents: Nicté is the flower of the Maya underworld deity Ix Tab (goddess of suicide and hanging). Despite or because of this association, its sap and leaves were used medicinally by Maya healers. Uses documented: latex for toothache (applied directly to tooth cavity), bark infusion for skin diseases, flower infusion for fever. The plant's dual nature — beautiful flower, bitter poisonous latex — exemplifies the Maya understanding that the most powerful medicines are often also the most dangerous. Convergence: Plumeria species are used medicinally across Mesoamerica (Maya, Aztec), South Asia (Ayurveda — champa), and Southeast Asia (Philippines). Multiple independent traditions independently identified medicinal properties. Modern: plumericin (iridoid) from Plumeria — documented antiprotozoal activity against Leishmania and Trypanosoma in laboratory studies.

label: ritual_bacabs::hmen_physician_role
text: H-men — The Maya Healer-Physician. Roys (1965) and Landa (1566, Relación de las Cosas de Yucatán): The h-men (from h, male, and men, maker/one who does) was the male Maya healer — female counterparts existed as ix-men. The h-men's role: diagnosis by pulse reading (distinct method from Chinese or Tibetan pulse reading — Maya pulse reading used different sites on the body), blood-letting (small cuts, not venesection), plant medicine prescription, and ritual incantation. Training: apprenticeship under an established h-men, often hereditary in families. Payment: traditional exchange in goods, not money. Coexistence: Spanish colonial records document the coexistence of Maya h-men with colonial Spanish and indigenous-Mexican physician-surgeons in Yucatán — the same competition of medical systems that Cobo documents in Peru. The h-men tradition continues today in Yucatán, Chiapas, and Guatemala, making Maya medicine one of the living traditions in this archive alongside Kampo, Ayurveda, and Tibetan medicine.

label: ritual_bacabs::ramon_brosimum
text: Ramon (Brosimum alicastrum) — Breadnut Tree / Maya Nut. Landa (1566) and archaeological evidence: Ramon seeds were a major caloric staple in Classic Maya civilization (250–900 CE) — some Maya sites show ramon seed stores larger than maize stores. Maya name: ox (in some regional dialects). Medicinal uses: leaves for cattle fodder and traditional human medicine (leaves as astringent poultice); bark decoction for asthma; seeds as anti-galactagogue (reduces milk flow). Nutritional profile now fully characterised: extraordinary amino acid profile (all essential amino acids), calcium higher than milk, magnesium higher than oats, tryptophan content higher than most foods. The tryptophan content is pharmacologically significant — it is the serotonin precursor, and traditional Maya uses of ramon (calming, anti-anxiety) may reflect genuine serotonergic activity through nutritional tryptophan loading. Now commercially available as a 'superfood' — another case of traditional food knowledge validated by modern nutritional science.
